channel.title: Lutheran Answers – Cryptid
channel.description: Recent content in Cryptid on Lutheran Answers

item.title: The Nephilim Were Not Supernatural Beings
item.description:
Introduction

  • The supernatural/angelic interpretation of the Nephilim (Genesis 6:1–4) has gained significant popular momentum in recent years
  • This article does not aim to be definitive — the text is brief and the question is genuinely difficult
    • Only two explicit references to the Nephilim in all of Scripture
    • The primary passage is only a few verses (Genesis 6:1–4)
  • Thesis: the textual, canonical, theological, and historical evidence leans toward a human genealogical interpretation — the “sons of God” as the faithful line of Seth, the “daughters of men” as the unfaithful line of Cain

The Text: Genesis 6:1–4

  • Read and briefly exposit the passage in full before arguing from it
  • Identify the two competing interpretations:
    • Supernatural view: “sons of God” = angels or fallen angelic/demonic beings who mate with human women, producing hybrid offspring (the Nephilim)
    • Genealogical/covenantal view: “sons of God” = faithful line of Seth; “daughters of men” = unfaithful line of Cain; intermarriage leads to spiritual corruption and the wickedness that precipitates the Flood
  • Note: this passage serves as the narrative bridge between the fall genealogies (Genesis 4–5) and the Flood (Genesis 6–9)

Reason 1: The Context and Purpose of Genesis as a Book

  • Genesis was authored by Moses for the people of Israel as they prepared to enter the Promised Land
    • The book is not merely abstract cosmic history — it speaks directly to Israel’s historical situation
    • Genealogies, narratives, and figures in Genesis consistently relate to the nations and peoples surrounding Israel
  • The recurring temptation throughout the entire Old Testament is Israel intermarrying with pagan peoples and being drawn into idolatry
    • This warning appears repeatedly: Deuteronomy 7, the period of the Judges, Solomon’s foreign wives, Ezra/Nehemiah, etc.
    • The intermarriage interpretation of Genesis 6 fits this theme exactly
  • The supernatural interpretation raises an obvious contextual problem: nowhere in the Old Testament is Israel warned against mating with demonic beings
    • What pastoral relevance would a story about angel-human hybrids have for the people entering Canaan?
    • The intermarriage warning, by contrast, is immediately relevant and pastorally urgent

Reason 2: Genesis Is a Book of Human Genealogies — Two Rival Seeds

  • From the moment of the Fall, Genesis establishes the framework of two rival genealogical lines
    • Genesis 3:15 — the protoevangelium: enmity between the seed of the serpent and the seed of the woman
    • These are not metaphors — they are literal human lines in conflict
  • The division of humanity into two lines is traced through the early chapters:
    • Line of Cain (Genesis 4): marked by wickedness, culminating in Lamech — a figure of violence and pride (“I have killed a man for wounding me… Lamech seventy-sevenfold”)
    • Line of Seth (Genesis 4:25–5): marked by faithfulness — “then men began to call on the name of the Lord”
  • The parallel naming in the two genealogies is significant:
    • Both lines have an Enoch and a Lamech — but the characters are reversed moral mirrors of one another
    • Cain’s Enoch founds a city (symbol of human pride); Seth’s Enoch walks with God and is taken to heaven
    • Cain’s Lamech boasts of murder; Seth’s Lamech is the father of Noah
  • Genesis 6 immediately follows Genesis 5 — the reader has just been walked through both genealogies
    • The most natural reading is that the author is continuing the two-seeds theme: the faithful line (sons of God) intermarries with the unfaithful line (daughters of men), and the result is catastrophic spiritual corruption
    • Introducing angels into this carefully constructed genealogical structure disrupts the entire literary logic of the book

Reason 3: The Supernatural View Requires Unscriptural Assumptions About Angels

  • Jesus teaches that in the resurrection, humans “neither marry nor are given in marriage” but are “like the angels” (Matthew 22:30)
    • The comparison implies that angels do not procreate — procreation is not part of angelic existence
  • Angels in Scripture can appear in bodily form (e.g., the visitors to Abraham in Genesis 18), but they are not embodied beings in the way humans are
    • They were all created simultaneously — there is no angelic reproduction
  • The angelic fall, while not described in extensive detail in Scripture, appears to have been a singular event — not an ongoing process of angels taking human wives
  • For the supernatural view to work, one must posit:
    • Angels (or fallen angels/demons) capable of biological reproduction with humans
    • The existence of genuine human-angel hybrid offspring
    • None of this is supported anywhere else in Scripture
  • It is methodologically problematic to derive a major theological claim about the nature of angels from one brief, obscure, disputed passage — especially when it contradicts the broader witness of Scripture

Reason 4: The Supernatural View Depends Too Heavily on Second Temple Literature

  • Much of the contemporary case for the supernatural interpretation leans on pseudepigraphal literature, especially the Book of Enoch
    • The Book of Enoch elaborates wildly on the Genesis 6 account, describing the “Watchers” (fallen angels) mating with women and producing the Nephilim
  • Jude 14–15 does quote from 1 Enoch — but this does not grant the book quasi-canonical status
    • New Testament authors could cite extra-biblical sources to make a point without endorsing the entire document (cf. Paul citing Greek poets in Acts 17)
    • Jude’s use of Enoch does not require acceptance of all Enochic traditions
  • The hermeneutical principle matters: allowing second temple Jewish interpretive traditions to be determinative for reading the Old Testament leads to conclusions that frequently conflict with the New Testament’s own handling of those texts
    • This is a principle worth examining carefully — where else does it lead?
  • Second temple sources are valuable for understanding what some Jews thought at the time of the New Testament; they are not a reliable guide to what the Old Testament actually teaches

Reason 5: The Supernatural View Is Not the Majority Position of the Church

  • Among early Christian writers (pre-4th century), the supernatural interpretation does appear
    • It is not a modern invention — but neither is it as universally ancient as sometimes claimed
  • By the time of Augustine (late 4th century), the supernatural interpretation largely disappears from prominent theological writing in both East and West
    • Augustine himself argues for the Sethite/genealogical interpretation
  • The current revival of interest in the supernatural/Enochic interpretation appears to be largely a recent phenomenon
    • Much of it is being driven by popular-level content and, in some cases, Eastern Orthodox sources — though even the Eastern tradition historically did not hold this as a majority position
  • The weight of the argument: departing from the historic consensus of the church requires strong exegetical and theological reasons
    • Reliance on second temple literature and the popularity of the view online do not constitute sufficient grounds

Conclusion

  • The Nephilim passage is genuinely brief and genuinely difficult — humility is appropriate on all sides
  • But the convergence of evidence is significant:
    • Authorial context and purpose of Genesis point to the intermarriage interpretation
    • The two-seeds genealogical structure of Genesis 4–5 sets up Genesis 6 as its natural conclusion
    • Scripture elsewhere gives no warrant for angel-human reproduction
    • The case for the supernatural view rests significantly on non-canonical Jewish sources
    • The historic majority of the church read this passage the other way
  • Practical/pastoral note: whatever one concludes about the Nephilim, the spiritual warning of the passage is clear — the corruption of the faithful through union with the unfaithful is catastrophic
    • This has immediate application for the life of the church today

Sources & References from the Video

Scripture

  • Genesis 3:15 (protoevangelium / two seeds)
  • Genesis 4:16–24 (line of Cain; Lamech’s boast)
  • Genesis 4:25–26 (line of Seth; calling on the name of the Lord)
  • Genesis 5 (genealogy of Seth)
  • Genesis 6:1–4 (the Nephilim passage)
  • Matthew 22:30 (angels do not marry or reproduce)
  • Jude 14–15 (citation of 1 Enoch)

Historical/Theological Figures Mentioned

  • Augustine of Hippo — credited with the shift away from the supernatural interpretation by the late 4th century

Extra-Biblical Literature Discussed

  • 1 Enoch (Book of Enoch) — pseudepigraphal; elaborates the “Watcher” tradition; cited by Jude but not thereby granted canonical authority

Additional Sources Worth Consulting for the Article

  • Luther’s Lectures on Genesis (LW 1–8) — Luther addresses Genesis 6 and the Sethite interpretation
  • Augustine, City of God, Book XV — treats the sons of God / daughters of men question directly
  • Lutheran Study Bible or confessional commentary on Genesis for pastoral framing
  • Relevant AALC/confessional Lutheran resources on the authority of Scripture vs. tradition (for the hermeneutics section)
